title: Zero Values
description: What happens when you don't give a variable a value?

slides.title: Empty Boxes 🪹
slides.content:
## Go Doesn't do "Null" Well

In many languages, if you create a variable but forget to give it a value, it becomes `undefined` or `null`, which causes programs to crash.

Go is safer. It gives uninitialized variables a **Zero Value** automatically!

quizData.question: What does Go do if you declare a variable without setting its value?

options.text: It gives the variable a default 'Zero Value'

quizData.explanation: Go prevents undefined errors by proactively assigning safe default values.

slides.title: The Zero Values 🛡️
slides.content:
Here are the automatic values Go assigns:
*   Number types (`int`, `float64`) get `0`.
*   Booleans (`bool`) get `false`.
*   Strings (`string`) get `""` (an empty string).

Let's prove it!
slides.codeSnippet:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    var number int
    var text string
    var isValid bool

    fmt.Println("Number:", number)
    // An empty string just prints nothing visible!
    fmt.Println("Text:", text)
    fmt.Println("Valid:", isValid)
}

quizData.question: If you declare `var isHappy bool` without a value, what does Go assign to it?
quizData.explanation: The zero value for any boolean in Go is automatically `false`.
